details.text:
الٓمٓصٓ

- These are from the Huroof Al-Muqatta’aat (الحُرُوفُ المُقَطَّعات), letters which are cut apart and separated from each other, with which some of the Suwar of the Qur’an begin with. The majority view is that none but Allah knows their meaning although some of the Sahabah and Scholars have provided some explanations for the meanings of these letters. One view is that these letters represent a challenge to the Arabs in respect to the Qur’an having been revealed in their language and yet they are unable to reproduce the like of it. It represents a challenge to their intellect.

Derivative: مَعَايِشَ (Ma’aayish) is the plural of مَعِيشَة (Ma’eeshah) and refers to the “means of living, subsisting and livelihood”. It comes from عِيْشَة and عَيْش which implies to live well. مَعَايِشَ (Ma’aayish) therefore implies “the means to live well or a good life”.
Derivative: مَكَّنَّا comes from مَكَّنَ – يُمَكِّنُ – تَمْكِين which means to consolidate, establish and make firm in a place, situation or setting.

Derivative: الْكَافِرِينَ is the plural of كَافِر (Kaafir) which comes from كَفَرَ – يَكْفُرُ – كُفْرًا and which means to “disbelieve” or “deny something”. Another meaning of كَفَرَ (Kafara) is to bury and cover something up and as such كَافِر (Kaafir) can imply “the one who buries or covers up the truth”.

Derivative: حَقِيقٌ (Haqeeq) means “worthy and deserving”. The meaning here can be “To be qualified and have the credibility” to not say about Allah except the truth. It has also been understood to mean “Obligated” and “duty-bound”.
Derivative: صَوَّرْنَا is from صَوَّرَ – يُصَوِّرُ – تَصْوِير which means to “fashion and mould” into a form.
Derivative: إِبْلِيسَ refers to Shaytan and is derived from أَبْلَسَ – يُبْلِسُ – إِبْلاس which means to be in a state of abject hopelessness and despair.

Derivative: حَاشِرِينَ is the plural of حَاشِر (Haashir) from حَشَرَ which means “to gather like cattle and livestock are herded”. حَاشِر (Haashir) is a “a gatherer” who gathers in a forceful or commanding manner, like a recruiter that gives no choice to the one he is recruiting.

Derivative: أَرْجِهْ comes from أَرْجَهَ which means to delay a matter from its time like the meaning of أَرْجَأَ and some have said that it’s the same word but the هَمْزَة (Hamzah – أ) has been exchanged for a هاء (Haa’ – ه).
Derivative: اسْتَرْهَبُو comes from اِسْتَرْهَبَ. رُهْبَة (Ruhbah) means “fright and terror” and relates to an immediate fear. اِسْتَرْهَبَ means to instil fright and terror into somebody.
Derivative: تَلْقَفُ is from لَقِفَ – يَلْقَفُ – لَقْفًا and means to grab, snatch and seize something quickly. Here it means to consume and swallow quickly.
Derivative: إِذْ implies the meaning of “at the time when” i.e., “when” in conjunction with immediacy.

Derivative: الصَّاغِرِينَ is the plural of صَاغِر (Saaghir) which means the one who has been made small, lowly and humiliated. To be characterised by صَغار (Saghaar) the state of lowliness and humiliation.
Derivative: السِّنِينَ is the plural of سَنَّة (Sannah) and was used by the Arabs to refer to a drought. It has been said to refer to years of drought and famine or a long period of it.

Derivative: يَطَّيَّرُوا comes from the origin of تَطَيَّرَ – يَتَطَيَّرٌ – تَطَيُّر but the Taa’ (ت) has been merged with the Taa’ (ط) to form a double Taa’ (ط) with a Shaddah (شَدَّة - ّ). تَطَيَّرَ comes from طَيْر (Tayr) which is “a bird” and especially birds like vultures and death. They became superstitious signs of bad luck and misfortune. تَطَيَّرَ here means to attribute bad luck and the occurrence of misfortune to someone.

Derivative: الطُّوفَانَ (Toufaan) comes from طَافَ – يَطُوفُ which means to go around and became used with water when crops, fields and homes are covered and overrun by torrents and rain. A طُوفَانَ (Toufaan) refers to such a flood.

Derivative: مُّفَصَّلَاتٍ is the plural of مُفَصًّلَة (Mufassalah) which comes from فَصَّلَ - تَفْصِيل (Tafseel) which can mean to explain something in detail and break it down into parts. مُّفَصَّلَاتٍ would consequently carry the meaning of “clear” i.e., “clear and plain Aayaat (signs)”. فَصَّلَ - تَفْصِيل (Tafseel) can also mean to separate and as such مُّفَصَّلَاتٍ can imply here that the Aayaat (signs) Allah sent against Fir’awn and his people were separated from each other and came one at a time.

Derivative: الرِّجْزُ is used in Arabic to refer to poetry that was chanted by soldiers to instil fear in those who heard it. It refers here to a punishment that leaves one psychologically battered and disturbed.

- “We will certainly without doubt give in (لَنُؤْمِنَنَّ) to you (لَكَ) (i.e., to your terms or demands)” – Here it is worth noting the difference between the verb آَمَنَ – يُؤْمِنُ when used with بِ and when used with لِ. When used with بِ (Bi) آَمَنَ (Aamana) means to believe in something or someone. When used with لِ (Li) it means “To give in to or to concede to something or someone”.

Derivative: يَنكُثُونَ is from نَكَثَ – يَنْكُثُ – نَكْث which means to break or violate a promise, contract or agreement and is the opposite of أَوْفَى which is used to mean to fulfil a promise, agreement or contract.
Derivative: الْيَمِّ (Al-Yamm) is used in the Qur’an to refer to large bodies of water like the river Nile or the Red Sea.
Derivative: أَنظِرْ is the command form from أَنْظَرَ – يُنْظِرُ – إِنْظَار and it means to give respite i.e., to give somebody some time to do something before preventing or punishing them.

Derivative: أَعْمَالُ (A’amaal) is the plural of عَمَل (‘Amal) which refers to “an action” or “deed”. When used in the Qur’an without further qualification indicating otherwise it implies “good deeds or acts”.

- It literally translates as: “It fell or dropped (سُقِطَ) in their hands (فِي أَيْدِيهِمْ)” – It the plural of the Arabic expression سُقِطَ فِي يَدِه “It fell in his hand” which carries the meaning of: “To be at loss and extremely regretful”.

Derivative: الْمُنظَرِينَ “Those who have been given respite i.e., time and a delay” is in the plural form even though the address is to Iblees in the singular form. It implies that he has followers who will join him in his pursuits.

Derivative: تُشْمِتْ comes from أَشْمَتَ – يُشْمِتُ – إِشْمات which means to cause somebody to take pleasure in the misfortune or mishap of others. In this context in means: Don’t cause the enemies “to take pleasure or rejoice” in my misfortune in the case where Musa (as) was openly chastising him (i.e., Harun (as)).
Derivative: عَزَّرُو comes from عَزَّرَ – يُعَزِّرُ – تَعْزِير and one of its meanings is “to go out of one’s way to show respect to someone”.

- “So, believe in Allah (فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ) and His Messenger (وَرَسُولِهِ) and the unlettered Prophet (النَّبِيِّ الْأُمِّيِّ)” – When two words which are similar in meaning are mentioned in the same statement, it implies that a difference between them should be found. Here رَسُولِ (Rasool) emphasises that he ﷺ came with a message for the people whilst النَّبِيِّ (An-Nabiy) highlights that he ﷺ brought news and information to the people.

Derivative: أَغْوَيْتَ is from أَغْوَى which originally comes from غَوَى – يَغْوِي – غِوَايَة / غَي has been understood to be lost and to stray or go astray from the right path like the word ضَلَّ (Dalla). However, the origin of the meaning of the word غَوَى is to lose one’s way because of an error. The meaning of أَغْوَى can therefore imply here “to have made me fall into error or transgression”. It can also imply an attempt here by Shaytan to shift blame for his sin.

Derivative: أَقْعُدَ comes from قَعَدَ – يَقْعُدُ – قُعُود and it means “to sit” however it differs from the word جَلَسَ – يَجْلِسُ- - جُلُوس in that قُعُود (Qu’ood) implies sitting for a long period of time whilst جُلُوس (Juloos) merely implies the act of sitting which could be for a second or longer. لَأَقْعُدَنَّ with the added emphasis of the لَام (Laam – لَ) at the beginning of the verb and the double emphasis of the نُون (Noon) with a شَدَّة (Shaddah) “نَّ” at the end of the verb implies that Shaytan is stating that he will surely “sit persistently and continuously lying in wait” i.e., to divert the people from Allah’s straight path.
Derivative: الْغَمَامَ (Al-Ghamaam) are the white clouds that offer shade and are not a sign of heavy rain or storms.

Derivative: انبَجَسَتْ is from اِنْبَجَسَ and some have said that it means the same as اِنْفَجَرَ which means “to gush or burst out”. Others have said that اِنْبَجَسَ implies less water than اِنْفَجَرَ and that it depicts water coming out from a narrow source. However, both words have been used to describe this incident in the Qur’an. That could mean that they mean the same or that one preceded the other i.e., a lesser amount followed by gushing forth in large amounts.
Derivative: بَئِيسٍ (Ba’ees) in reference to punishment means “severe, painful and terrible”.

Derivative: خَاسِئِينَ is the plural of خَاسِئ (Khaasi’) and comes from the verb خَسِئَ which means to driven away. The verb is used as an expression in Arabic to mean “Scram” or “Get away from here”. خَاسِئ (Khaasi’) can carry the meaning of being rejected, outcast, low and despicable.
Derivative: تَأَذَّنَ is used when a declaration or proclamation is being made.

Derivative: الْمُصْلِحِينَ is from أَصْلَحَ – يُصْلِحُ – إِصْلَاح which means to rectify, reform and put something right. صَالِح (Saalih) is the person who puts himself right and is righteous whilst مُصْلِح (Muslih) is the one who makes other than him good.
Derivative: انسَلَخَ is used to describe the act of a snake shedding its skin. It means to shed, cast or strip something off, or give something up and abandon it.

Derivative: الْغَاوِينَ is the plural of الغاوي (Al-Ghaawiy) which is from the verb غَوَى – يَغْوِي – غَيّ which means stray from the right way or path. الْغَاوِينَ refers to those who took the wrong path and then kept going upon it.

Derivative: مَّدْحُورًا (Mad’hoor) comes from دَحَرَ which carries two meanings. One of the meanings is to expel in the case where مَدْحُور (Mad’hoor) means “Expelled” and implies distance i.e., to be expelled far away like being banished or exiled from a land.

Derivative: مَذْءُومًا (Madh’oom) comes from ذَأَمَ – يَذْأَمُ – ذَأْمًا and carries the same meaning as ذَمَّ – يَذُمُّ – ذَمًًّا which means to reproach or find blameworthy. In addition to these meanings, ذَأَمَ also implies a stronger level of reproach and can be understood to mean “to find contemptible or to be disgusted by something or someone”. مَذْءُومًا (Madh’oom) therefore implies the meaning of “Contemptible” or “Disgraced”.
Derivative: يُلْحِدُونَ comes from أَلْحَدَ – يُلْحِدُ – إِلْحَاد and means to deviate and digress.

Derivative: نَسْتَدْرِجُ comes from اِسْتَدْرَجَ - يَسْتَدْرِجُ – اِسْتِدْرَاج which means to lead someone gradually or level by level to what is intended. It can mean here to lead them to their destruction bit by bit and also to open avenues for them in the life of this world bit by bit where they are deceived into thinking that they are making progress whilst in reality sealing their fate.
Derivative: مَتِينٌ (Mateen) is something that is firm and strong. A knot that is مَتِينٌ (Mateen) implies that it can’t be undone.
Derivative: صَاحِب (Saahib) means "companion" and implies intimate knowledge of someone through close and lengthy interaction and companionship.
Derivative: حَفِيٌّ (Hafiy) refers to someone who is a persistent enquirer about a matter.
Derivative: يُجَلِّي is from جَلَّى – تَجْلِيَة and it means to make something clear, to reveal and to bring to light.

Derivative: ثَقُلَتْ means to “weigh heavy” or “be a heavy burden” and conjures the image of something that needs to unload the burden and weight when the final hour arrives. It has also been said that this “weightiness” and “burden” is in reference to the inhabitants of the heavens and earth in relation to the coming of the final hour.

Derivative: مُرْسَا is the Masdar (Meemiy) of أَرْسَى which means to fasten, anchor or put something in place firmly. The verb is usually used for things of great weight and magnitude like mountains being pegged in the earth. Here it carries the meaning of final hour being “settled” and “taking hold”. It is in reference to its “arrival”.
Derivative: يَبْطِشُونَ comes from بَطَشَ – بَطْش which means to grab and seize something by strength and taking it.

وَتَرَاهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ

- “And you see them (وَتَرَاهُمْ) looking (يَنظُرُونَ) at you (إِلَيْكَ) whilst they (وَهُمْ) are not seeing (لَا يُبْصِرُونَ)” – This part of the Aayah uses all three verbs related to vision: رَأَى which means “To see” something in a general manner, نَظَرَ which means “To look” at something and أَبْصَرَ which means “To see with full sight” where you take in and fully grasp or comprehend what you are seeing.
Derivative: وَسْوَسَ implies the meaning of whispering by sneaking up from behind and then disappearing in a repeated manner.

Derivative: يَنزَغَ is from نَزَغَ – يَنْزَغُ – نَزْغًا means to incite and prod something. To be goaded into something. It is usually used for inciting trouble and here it refers to Shaytan’s prompting and inciting you to do something.
Derivative: أَنصِتُوا is the plural command form from أَنْصَتَ and means “to listen” but implies to be quiet and listen.
Derivative: اسْتَمِعُوا is the plural command form from اِسْتَمَعَ and it means “to listen” to something attentively. It differs to سَمِعَ which just means “to hear”.
Derivative: قَاسَمَ – يُقَاسِمُ – مُقَاسَمَة refers to emphatically swear something and is used when swearing a strong or binding oath.
Derivative: النَّاصِحِينَ is the plural of نَاصِح which is the person that gives نَصِيحَة (Naseehah) which refers to an “advice” that benefits the person it is being given to.
Derivative: سَوْآتُ is the plural of سَوْءَة (Saw’ah) and indicates to a source of shame and here it refers to one’s عَوْرَة (‘Awrah) i.e., nakedness.
Derivative: يَخْصِفَانِ is from خَصَفَ – يَخْصِفُ – خَصْف which means to stick, patch and attach something on top of something.
Derivative: طَفِقَا is the dual form from طَفِقَ which means to begin to do something or to take to doing something.

Derivative: دَلَّا / دَلَّى which linguistically means to make something descend, fall down or to bring something down or lower it. It is also used to imply the meaning of convincing somebody over time to do something i.e., to gradually lure a person into doing something.

Derivative: رِيشًا (Reesh) in origin means “feathers” and when used to describe clothing it implies its beauty like the feather of the bird is a sign of its beauty. In this context it implies the meaning of as an “adornment” or as a “means to beautify oneself”.

- “The clothing (لِبَاسُ) of Taqwaa (التَّقْوَىٰ)” – This statement can be understood literally in the case that the clothing that preserves Taqwaa is the best type of clothing. It can also be understood figuratively in the case where it means the covering of Taqwaa i.e., to be characterised by it.
Derivative: مَسْجِدٍ (Masjid) is a ظَرْف (Zharf - Adverb) and literally means the place or time of سُجُود (Sujood – Prostrating) but implies the meaning of the place or time of prayer.

Derivative: تُسْرِفُوا is from أَسْرَفَ – يُسْرِفُ – إِسْراف and it means to be excessive i.e., to go beyond the Halal and indulging in what is Haram. In addition to that it has also been said to refer to being over extravagant and excessive in respect to that which is Halal, like eating too much, buying too many things or using too much water to a level that is well beyond your needs, and which leads to waste.
- “Eat (كُلُوا) and drink (وَاشْرَبُوا)” – This is an expression which means to enjoy the good and Halal blessings of life.
Derivative: بَطَنَ means to be hidden and concealed under or inside something.
Derivative: ادَّارَكُوا is from اِدَّارَكَ which in origin is تَدَارَكَ which means when some catch up and meet with others.
Derivative: قَائِلُونَ is the plural of قَائِل (Qaa’il) which comes from قَالَ – يَقِيلُ – قَيْلُولَة and means to take a midday nap or siesta.
Derivative: تُفَتَّحُ is the passive form from فَتَّحَ – تَفْتِيح which is a verb that describes the process of opening like a flower opening up or how a door opens.

- “Until (حَتَّىٰ) the camel (الْجَمَلُ) enters (يَلِجَ) through (فِي) the eye (سَمِّ) of the needle (الْخِيَاطِ)” – This is an Arabic expression to highlight the absolute impossibility of something happening.
details.arabic_part: حَتَّىٰ يَلِجَ الْجَمَلُ فِي سَمِّ الْخِيَاطِ

details.text: Derivative: غَوَاشٍ is the plural of غَاشِيَة and it is the thing that covers something. غَوَاشٍ (Ghawaashin) means layers of covers or coverings.
Derivative: غِلٍّ (Ghill) refers to the secret hatred, animosity and ill feeling that somebody harbours within himself towards somebody or something.
Derivative: يَطْمَعُونَ comes from طَمَعَ – يَطْمَعُ – طَمْعًا which means to covet something and long for it i.e., have a deep and strong desire to have or attain something.

Derivative: الْأَعْرَافِ (Al-A’araaf) is the plural of عُرْف (‘Urf) which refers to an elevated place. عُرْف (‘Urf) is a place that you can recognise everybody and الْأَعْرَافِ (Al-A’araaf) refers to “heights” where everybody has vision.

Derivative: تَأْوِيلَ (Ta’weel) from أَوَّلَ - يُؤَوِّلُ – تَأْوِيلًا means to interpret and explain something. It comes from آلَ – يَؤُولُ which carries the meaning to return. From this meaning تَأْوِيل (Ta’weel) can carry the meaning of something’s outcome, consequence, result and place of return i.e., where something returns to and its end point. It can be understood here to mean in the context of the Verse: Are they waiting for anything besides “its consequence to be manifested”.

Derivative: حَثِيثًا (Hatheeth) comes from حَثَّ – يَحُثُّ – حَثًّا which means to spur something on and is used to indicate to hurry something and bring it on quickly. حَثِيثًا (Hatheeth) carries the meaning of “quickly” and “rapidly”.

Derivative: مُسَخَّرَاتٍ is the plural of مُسَخَّر (Musakh’khar) which is derived from سَخَّرَ – يُسَخِّرُ - تَسْخِير which means to “subdue” and “subject” something i.e., to make something under something’s or someone’s control and disposal. مُسَخَّرَاتٍ (Musakh’kharaat) means that the sun and the moon and the stars are “subdued” and “subservient” to His command.

Derivative: تَضَرُّعًا (Tadarru’) combines the meanings of خُشُوع (Khusoo’) which is humbleness or humility and خُضُوع (Khudoo’) which is subservience and surrender. It therefore combines both spiritual and physical elements.

إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ قَرِيبٌ مِّنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ

“No doubt the mercy of Allah (إِنَّ رَحْمَتَ اللَّهِ) is close (قَرِيبٌ) to those who excel (مِّنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ)” – Here it is observed that قَرِيبٌ (Qareeb – close/near) is masculine and not feminine despite following رَحْمَتَ (Rahmah - mercy) which is feminine. It is said that this is because the قَرِيبٌ (Qareeb – close/near) is being linked to Allah as well. That is to highlight that Allah and not just His mercy is close to the Muhsineen (those who excel).

Derivative: أَقَلَّتْ is from أَقَلَّ – يُقِلُّ – إِقْلَال and it can mean to consider something little. In respect to the winds it implies that it considers the heavy clouds to be insignificant and moves them with ease. It is due to this that أَقَلَّ is also used to imply the meaning of raising, lifting and carrying something.

Derivative: نَكِدًا (Nakid) implies the meanings of “giving or producing a little” and also “difficulty and hardship”. The meaning of نَكِدًا (Nakid) here in relation to crops and vegetation coming out of the bad land or soil can include all these meanings: “little in quantity, of little to no benefit and difficult to attain despite efforts invested”.
Derivative: خَبُثَ means that which is or becomes خَبِيث (Khabeeth) which is understood to refer to something that has no good in it.

Derivative: أَنصَحُ comes from نَصَحَ – يَنْصَحُ – نُصْح which means to give good and sincere counsel and advice. It has been said to refer to “the speech through which goodness for someone other than yourself is intended”.

Derivative: غَائِبِينَ is the plural of غَائِب (Ghaa’ib) which comes from غَابَ – يَغِيبُ which means to not be seen and by implication means to be absent or not present. غَائِبِينَ (Ghaa’ibeen) here means “absent”.

Derivative: نَقُصَّ is from قَصَّ – يَقُصُّ which means to narrate about something elaborately. حَكَى – يَحْكِي – حِكَايَة means to tell or relate a story whilst قَصَّ means to narrate a story in an elaborate fine manner with details.

- قَطَعَ دَابِرَ الشَيْءِ “He cut off the Daabir (root) of something” is an Arabic expression to mean to completely eradicate something and to root it out, in the case where one of the meanings of دَابِر (Daabir) is “a root”.
Derivative: تَعْثَوْا comes from عَثِيَ – يَعْثَى – عُثِيًّا which means to go about and cause great mischief and corruption.
Derivative: بَوَّأَ means to provide someone with a place to settle in or a position to take where everything is facilitated for you in that.

Derivative: اسْتُضْعِفُوا is the passive form of اِسْتَضْعَفَ which implies the meaning of seeking to keep someone weak and powerless. اسْتُضْعِفُوا means those “who were attempted to be kept weak and powerless” i.e., those who were subdued, repressed and oppressed.

Derivative: الَّذِي is an Ism ul-Mawsool (relative pronoun) meaning “what or which” and differs from مَا (Maa) in the case where الَّذِي (Al-Ladhee) relates to something specific whilst مَا (Maa) implies generality with the meaning of “whatever”.
Derivative: عَتَوْا comes from عَتَا – يَعْتُو – عُتُوًا which means “to rebel” when it is done to show defiance. It is when you defy and transgress against set bounds in an arrogant manner.

Derivative: الرَّجْفَةُ (Ar-Rajfah) comes from رَجَفَ – يَرْجُفُ – رَجْف and it means to convulse, shake and agitate. رَجْفَة (Rajfah) is an event of convulsion, major disturbance or destabilisation, and is used to describe a severe earthquake.

Derivative: جَاثِمِينَ is the plural of جَاثِم (Jaathim) that comes from جَثَمَ which means to lie prone i.e., flat out on one’s chest. It is used to depict the image of bodies lying scattered all over the place.

Derivative: يَتَطَهَّرُونَ comes from تَطَهَّرَ – يَتَطَهَّرُ – تَطَهُّر which is origin means to purify oneself and is used to mean to consider oneself to be pure and above partaking in certain practises.
Derivative: الْغَابِرِينَ is the plural of غَابِر (Ghaabir) which comes from غَبَرَ which means to remain. الْغَابِرِينَ means: “Those who remained” i.e., stayed behind.
Derivative: تَبْخَسُوا comes from بَخَسَ – يَبْخَسُ – بَخْس and in origin in means to “reduce” and to “diminish”. Its meaning here is to give less than due or deprive them of that.
Derivative: تُوعِدُونَ comes from أَوْعَدَ – يُوْعِدُ – إِيعَاد which means to threaten, menace and intimidate.

Derivative: مِلَّتِ or مِلَّة (Millah) is one of the words used in Arabic to refer to a religion and to a nation. مِلَّة (Millah) implies a nation that is unified by a religion or something that is devoted to like a religion and has an idea, leader or leadership that is committed to.
Derivative: تَعُودُ comes from عَادَ – يَعُودُ – عَوْدَة which means “to return to where you started i.e., one’s original position”.
Derivative: كَارِهِينَ is the plural of كَارِه (Kaarih) which means to be unwilling and averse to do something. It is used when somebody is being forced or pressured to do something against his will.

Derivative: افْتَحْ is from فَتَحَ which means “to open”. It is also used to mean “to pass judgement” between two things or peoples in the case where “opening” implies removing the closed situation or status of a matter and judgement removes a problem that exists. The meaning here of the command افْتَحْ (Iftah) is “Pass judgement” between us and our Qawm (nation/people).

Derivative: يَغْنَوْا is from غَنِيَ – يَغْنَى – غِناء and means to be rich and free of want. When used in conjunction with a place it means to live and reside in it in a prosperous state where wealth is apparent.
Derivative: آسَىٰ is the first-person form from أَسَى – يَأْسَى – أَسًا which means to grieve and be sad.
Derivative: عَفَوا is from عَفَا – يَعْفُو which carries the meaning of to increase, become many and multiply.
Derivative: بَغْتَةً (Baghtah) means “suddenly and without warning”.
